---
pagetitle: "F1Tenth Autonomous Racing Stack"
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    page-layout: full
---

::: {.hero-section}

# F1Tenth Autonomous Racing Stack {.title}

::: {.subtitle}
Full-stack autonomous racing with on-the-fly SLAM, raceline optimization, and visual-inertial odometry
:::

::: {.author-list}

[**Ravi Girish**](https://github.com/thebirdgr)^1^,
[**Teammate 2**](https://example.com)^1^,
[**Teammate 3**](https://example.com)^1^

:::

::: {.affiliation-list}

^1^University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign — ECE 484: Robot Perception

:::

::: {.button-row}

[[{{< fa brands github >}} Code]{.btn-text}](https://github.com/thebirdgr){.btn .btn-primary}

:::

:::


<!-- ============================================================ -->
<!-- TEASER IMAGE / VIDEO -->
<!-- ============================================================ -->

::: {.section-container}

::: {.hero-teaser}

<!-- Option A: Use a static image as the teaser -->
![System overview: disparity extender reactive control during the mapping lap (left), pure pursuit on the optimized raceline during race laps (right).](static/images/placeholder-teaser.svg){.teaser-img}

<!-- Option B: Embed a video teaser (uncomment below, comment out image above)
{{< video static/videos/teaser.mp4 >}}
-->

:::

:::


<!-- ============================================================ -->
<!-- ABSTRACT -->
<!-- ============================================================ -->

::: {.section-container}

## Abstract {.section-title}

::: {.abstract-text}
We present a full autonomous racing stack for the F1Tenth platform targeting competitive lap times on unknown tracks. The core challenge is that the track layout is not known ahead of time, requiring the vehicle to simultaneously build a map and navigate safely before switching to an optimized racing strategy. Our approach uses a two-phase pipeline: during the first lap, a **disparity extender** reactive controller safely navigates the unknown track while **slam_toolbox** builds a 2D occupancy map in the background. Once the map is complete, we switch to **AMCL localization** against the saved map and execute **pure pursuit** on a minimum-curvature raceline computed offline using a quadratic programming formulation. Velocity at each waypoint is optimized subject to friction circle and actuator constraints using **CVXPY**. As the primary ECE 484 contribution, we evaluate **visual-inertial odometry** (OpenVINS on the Intel RealSense D435i) as an alternative and complementary localization modality to LiDAR, measuring pose drift, failure modes at speed, and the benefit of EKF fusion of both sources. Our system runs fully on ROS2 Humble with a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR and RealSense D435i camera.
:::

:::


<!-- ============================================================ -->
<!-- OVERVIEW / METHOD VIDEO -->
<!-- ============================================================ -->

::: {.section-container}

## Video {.section-title}

::: {.video-container}
<!-- Replace with your YouTube or local video embed -->
{{< video static/videos/simulation_wall_follow.webm >}}
:::

:::


<!-- ============================================================ -->
<!-- WORKFLOW -->
<!-- ============================================================ -->

::: {.section-container}

## Development Workflow {.section-title}

::: {.content-text}

Our development follows three sequential stages, each building on the last:

**Stage 1 — SLAM Validation**
Run slam_toolbox in online async mode while the disparity extender drives a mapping lap. Verify loop closure by inspecting the saved occupancy grid. Key checks: map resolution (0.05 m/cell), wall continuity, and absence of ghost walls from poor scan matching. Once the map is clean and consistent across multiple mapping runs, we lock it and move to Stage 2.

**Stage 2 — Raceline Optimization**
Extract the track centerline from the saved `.pgm` map using contour detection. Feed the centerline waypoints into the minimum-curvature QP optimizer to generate the raceline. Then solve the CVXPY velocity profile subject to lateral acceleration limits ($v^2 \kappa \leq \mu g$) and actuator constraints. Validate by overlaying the raceline on the map and checking that all waypoints stay within track boundaries with sufficient clearance.

**Stage 3 — Controller Tuning**
Load the saved map, initialize AMCL localization, and run pure pursuit on the optimized raceline. Tune the lookahead distance (speed-adaptive: $L = k \cdot v$), the velocity profile scaling factor, and the PID gains for longitudinal speed control. Iterate on lap time vs. crash rate. Stretch goal: replace pure pursuit with MPCC using acados for online trajectory optimization.

:::

:::


<!-- ============================================================ -->
<!-- RESULTS GALLERY -->
<!-- ============================================================ -->

::: {.section-container}

## Results {.section-title}

::: {.content-text}
*Results coming soon — currently in Stage 1 (SLAM validation).*
:::

:::


<!-- ============================================================ -->
<!-- QUALITATIVE COMPARISONS -->
<!-- ============================================================ -->

::: {.section-container}

## Qualitative Comparisons {.section-title}

::: {.content-text}
*Comparisons coming soon — LiDAR AMCL vs. OpenVINS VIO localization at speed.*
:::

:::


<!-- ============================================================ -->
<!-- SYSTEM OVERVIEW -->
<!-- ============================================================ -->

::: {.section-container}

## System Overview {.section-title}

::: {.content-text}

Our stack operates in two phases:

**Phase 1 — Explore (Lap 1):** The disparity extender identifies the largest free gap in the 270° LiDAR scan, extends obstacle boundaries by the car's half-width to prevent wall hugging, and steers toward the gap midpoint. slam_toolbox runs concurrently in online async mode to build a 2D occupancy map. Once loop closure is detected, the map is saved.

**Phase 2 — Exploit (Lap 2+):** AMCL localizes the car against the saved map. The centerline is extracted from the occupancy grid and passed to a minimum-curvature QP optimizer to generate a raceline. A CVXPY velocity profile enforces lateral acceleration limits ($v^2 \kappa \leq \mu g$) and actuator constraints at each waypoint. Pure pursuit tracks this raceline with speed-adaptive lookahead distance.

**Sensors:** Hokuyo UST-10LX (270° FOV, 40Hz) · Intel RealSense D435i (RGB-D + IMU)

**Software:** ROS2 Humble · slam_toolbox · nav2 AMCL · robot_localization · OpenVINS · CVXPY

:::

:::

<!-- ============================================================ -->
<!-- TOOLS & EXPERIMENTS -->
<!-- ============================================================ -->

::: {.section-container}

## Tools & Planned Experiments {.section-title}

::: {.content-text}

**Mapping & Localization**

| Tool | Role | Status |
|---|---|---|
| slam_toolbox | Online async SLAM, loop closure | In progress |
| nav2 AMCL | Map-based localization for race laps | Planned |
| robot_localization | EKF fusion of LiDAR + IMU + VIO | Planned |
| OpenVINS | Visual-inertial odometry on D435i | Planned |

**Planning & Control**

| Tool | Role | Status |
|---|---|---|
| TUM Raceline Optimizer | Minimum-curvature QP raceline | Planned |
| CVXPY | Velocity profile optimization | Planned |
| Pure Pursuit | Baseline waypoint tracking controller | In progress |
| acados / CasADi | MPCC online optimization (stretch goal) | Stretch |

**Experiments**

1. **SLAM quality vs. speed** — does driving faster during the mapping lap degrade map quality enough to hurt localization on race laps?
2. **Raceline vs. centerline** — quantify lap time improvement from the optimized raceline over naive centerline following.
3. **LiDAR vs. VIO localization** — compare AMCL and OpenVINS pose drift at 2, 4, and 6 m/s. Does EKF fusion of both outperform either alone?
4. **Pure pursuit vs. MPCC** — if time permits, compare lap times and tracking error between the two controllers on the same raceline.

:::

:::


<!-- ============================================================ -->
<!-- RELATED WORK -->
<!-- ============================================================ -->

::: {.section-container}

## Related Work {.section-title}

::: {.content-text}

- [Heilmeier et al. (2020)](https://github.com/TUMFTM/global_racetrajectory_optimization) present the TUM global raceline optimizer, a minimum-curvature QP formulation we adopt for offline raceline generation.
- [Stahl et al. (2019)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.05150) introduce minimum-time lap optimization with friction circle constraints, motivating our CVXPY velocity profile.
- [Geneva et al. (2020)](https://github.com/rpng/open_vins) present OpenVINS, the open-source VIO system we use for camera-based localization on the D435i.
- [Macenski et al. (2021)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2109.07636) describe slam_toolbox, our mapping and localization backend.
- [Sezer & Gokasan (2012)](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/6315217) introduce the Follow the Gap reactive algorithm our disparity extender builds on.

:::

:::


<!-- ============================================================ -->
<!-- BIBTEX -->
<!-- ============================================================ -->

::: {.section-container}

## BibTeX {.section-title}

```bibtex
@article{girish2026f1tenth,
  author    = {Ravi Girish and Teammate Two and Teammate Three},
  title     = {F1Tenth Autonomous Racing Stack: On-the-Fly SLAM, Raceline Optimization, and Visual-Inertial Odometry},
  journal   = {ECE 484 Course Project, University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign},
  year      = {2026},
}
```

:::


<!-- ============================================================ -->
<!-- FOOTER -->
<!-- ============================================================ -->

::: {.site-footer}

This website template is adapted from the
[Nerfies](https://nerfies.github.io) project page, which is licensed under a
[Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International License](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/).

:::
